participants.extracted: The study enrolled 2435 children from intervention communities in the Dandé health district of southwestern Burkina Faso. Eligible participants were 8.80–9.99 months old at enrolment, permanent residents of the district, and had caregivers willing to remain available for the study period and accept home visits. Of the enrolled children, 2364 had at least 30 days of morbidity observation and were included in the final analyses. Mean age at enrolment was 9.4 months, and the sample included both boys and girls (about half male).

interventions.extracted: Children in the intervention cohort were cluster-randomized at the concession level to receive one of four daily zinc/nutrition supplement regimens from 9 to 18 months of age: (1) small-quantity lipid-based nutrient supplements (SQ-LNS) without added zinc plus a placebo tablet (LNS-Zn0), (2) SQ-LNS with 5 mg zinc plus a placebo tablet (LNS-Zn5), (3) SQ-LNS with 10 mg zinc plus a placebo tablet (LNS-Zn10), or (4) SQ-LNS without added zinc plus a 5 mg dispersible zinc tablet daily (LNS-TabZn5). The SQ-LNS was identical across groups except for zinc content and was designed to be given in two servings per day, preferably mixed with food, while the dispersible tablet was to be taken at least 30 minutes away from meals and SQ-LNS. Supplements were distributed weekly and adherence was monitored through caregiver report and return of unused products.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure morbidity outcomes during weekly community-based surveillance from 9 to 18 months of age (approximately 9 months of follow-up). Primary outcomes were the longitudinal prevalence of diarrhoea and malaria. Secondary morbidity outcomes included incidence and longitudinal prevalence of diarrhoea, malaria, fever, non-malaria fever, acute upper respiratory illness (AURI), acute lower respiratory illness (ALRI), and severe forms of these illnesses. Diarrhoea was defined as three or more liquid or semiliquid stools in 24 hours; severity included dehydration, faecal blood, six or more stools in 24 hours, or duration of at least 14 days. Fever was defined by caregiver report in the past 24 hours or measured auricular temperature of at least 37.5°C; non-malaria fever was fever without a positive malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) during the episode or within 2 days of it. Malaria was defined as fever with a positive RDT, and severe malaria included seizures, unconsciousness, or respiratory distress. AURI was defined as cough with purulent nasal discharge, and ALRI as cough with respiratory difficulty (wheezing/stridor or chest indrawing). The study also planned to record safety outcomes over follow-up, specifically serious adverse events defined as hospitalisation or death, and deaths.
